titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Manly Family Papers
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Martha Lund Smalley

conventiondeclaration.abbr: finding aid prepared according to local divinity library descriptive practices.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday August 19, 2020 at 22:03

did.abstract: Correspondence, writings, photographs, and collected materials document the life and work of the Manly family in China. Political unrest in Chengdu 1911-1912 is well documented, as is the daily work of a Methodist missionary working with native churches. The collection includes valuable photographs of Sichuan from the early 1900s. Wilson Edward Manly, his wife Florence and daughters Marian and Grace were American Methodist missionaries in Sichuan (Szechwan) province from 1893 to 1943.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Manly Family Papers (RG 82), Special Collections, Yale Divinity School Library.

chronitemset.event: Wilson Edward Manly went to China as Methodist missionary
Wilson Edward ("Ward") Manly and Florence Brown married in Shanghai, moved to Chengdu
Daughter Marian born in Chongqing (Chungking); Marian returned to China as medical doctor; she was also an author using the pseudonym Han Suyin.
Daughter Grace born in Chongqing (Chungking); returned to China as missionary; died in 1942.
W.E. Manly received D.D. from Cornell College

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I, Correspondence, includes detailed original letters written by W. E. Manly to his family in the U.S. from 1911 to 1914. A political uprising in Chengdu, dealings with native teachers and churches, and Methodist mission politics are described. Correspondence with Bishop J. W. Bashford,Joseph Beech, and other missionaries relates to the political unrest. Circular letters written by the Manlys to supporters in the U.S. provide a good overview of their work. A set of excerpts from Manly correspondence called "Chronicles" provides a glimpse of correspondence early than the letters available in original format.

Series II, Writings, includes material written by W. E. Manly, Grace Manly, and Marian Manly. W. E. Manly was interested in engineering devices to improve life in West China, including a waterwheel system to produce electricity. An essay by Marian Manly describes the Chin I School of Midwifery, which she headed.

Series III, Photographs, includes portraits and group shots of missionaries, Chinese native workers, conferences, and school groups, as well as scenery and scenes from life in Sichuan province in the early 1900s. Many of the photographs are identified, with approximate dates.
Series IV, Collected Material and Memorabilia, includes ephemeral written material and artifacts.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Place names were modernized in the description, with the name originally used in the collection material or in an older version of the finding aid in parenthesis: e.g. "Beijing (Peking)" or "Benin (Dahomey)".

did.unittitle: W.E. Manly from Chongqing (Chungking), Tzechow, and elsewhere in Sichuan to family in Ohio, including re: Methodist conference
W.E. Manly from Tzechow, Chongqing (Chungking), and elsewhere to family, including re: Bible School, trips to outlying congregations, political unrest in Chengdu (Chentu), Pa Gao secret society
W.E. Manly from Tzechow and elsewhere to family, including re: robberies, railroads
W.E. Manly from Tzechow and elsewhere to family, including re: trips to countryside, electrical power, "white slavery"
W.E. Manly from Tzechow and elsewhere to family, including re: salaries of native pastors, mission property
W.E. Manly from Tzechow and elsewhere to family; including re: native teacher playing cards, political unrest in Chongqing (Chungking)
W.E. Manly from Tzechow and elsewhere to family, including re: statistics of converts; return home on furlough (1914); return to China with Florence and Gordon
W.E. Manly to siblings and father
W.E. Manly to others, including Bishop J. W. Bashford, Joseph Beech, J. E. Johnson, re: insurrection in Yang Shien and Chengdu (Chentu), mission finances
Florence Manly to W.E. Manly and daughters
Circular letters to "Friends of the Decatur District," "Friends," including re: political unrest
To/from W.E. Manly: American Consular Service, including re: political unrest
To W.E. Manly: Bashford, Beech, Alice Brethorst, including re: "Revolutionists"
Letters from others to W.E. Manly: Cornell College re: D.D. conferred on W.E. Manly in absentia
To/from W.E. Manly: T. James, Spencer Lewis, S. McCartney, E. Meuser, J. Nicolson
To/from W.E. Manly: H. Openshaw, C. B. Rape, J. Sparling, Thomas Torrance
Letters from others to Florence Manly
Marian Manly to/from others, including to Sen. Fulbright, to President Nixon re: Vietnam War
Joseph Beech to Bishop Bashford re: uprising in Chengdu (Chentu)
Cables; inventory of correspondence prepared by Marian Manly
News updates re: political situation, etc.
"A Visit to the Salt Wells"; "Thoughts on Homegoing"
Re: Chin I School of Midwifery
Portraits and family groups: W.E. and Florence
Portraits and family groups: W.E. and Florence in groups
Portraits and family groups: Grace and Marian
Chinese associates, including native pastors and families
Methodist missionaries in West China; China Inland Mission missionaries in Chinese clothing
Missionaries; including vacationing in Sichuan (Szechuan), travel in countryside
Missionaries and Chinese workers - tribes country ("Thibetans")
Various mission projects, including babies born at Chin I School of Midwifery, native pastors
Tien Gu Chao Methodist station (destroyed by Boxers, then rebuilt)
W.E. Manly's waterwheel project for electricity
Sichuan (Szechuan) scenes - cities, Jin Fu San (Mountain of Golden Happiness)
Life of the people of Sichuan (Szechuan)
Life of the people of Sichuan (Szechuan)
Life of the people of Sichuan (Szechuan)
Life of the people of Sichuan (Szechuan)
Mr. and Mrs. Hudson Taylor, China Inland Mission missionaries
New Testament and Bible of Marian Manly
